author: @jordanbenaderet7929
text:
Dear Marques Brownlee,

Thermal Testing Idea for iPhone 16 Pro vs 17 Pro. If anyone could figure out how to test this properly, it would be you and your team! 

Please read below:
I’d love to see a thermal comparison between the iPhone 16 Pro from last year—with its custom built-in vapor chamber—to the new iPhone 17 Pro, which uses aluminum with a vapor chamber. I’m really curious if the gains are actually that much greater, and whether Apple’s claims about aluminum being just as effective hold up. To me, it seems like a real trade-off in durability and rigidity.

For context, I’ve owned almost every iPhone since the very first one, and I’ve almost never used a case. But this year feels different. With the weight and softness of the aluminum frame, even a short drop onto a hard surface could leave a noticeable nick or dent. For the first time, I actually feel like I need a case.

That leads to another question: if using a case is the “solution,” how does that affect thermal performance? A case essentially adds another barrier, which seems like it would trap heat—or at least slow down heat dissipation through the aluminum shell.

author: @thetempterbutnoop7273
text:
Hi, I’m watching you from Turkey. Here we pay a lot of taxes, and it really affects phone prices. For example, the iPhone 17 costs about $1,885, the 16 Pro is $2,000, while a Samsung Galaxy S25 is around $1,100. I’m currently using a Samsung S20 FE, which I bought about three years ago for roughly $450–550.

This tax system makes things tough, but I want to ask you something: based on normal (international) pricing, are these devices really worth buying? I mainly use my phone for social media content creation, taking photos, and browsing websites. If I don’t consider it an investment, is spending this much money on a phone still a good idea—Lmfao?

By the way, I’m a university student, so I handle many daily tasks on my phone, and good battery life is a lifesaver. Thank you for your great videos and voiceovers—I used a translator to write this, so I hope it’s clear

author: @reldegartGi
text:
There is a practical battery size limit for smartphones sold in the USA due to regulations for transporting lithium-ion batteries, specifically 49 CFR 173.185, which limits each cell to a 20Wh (Watt-hour) capacity for ground transport. While there isn't a direct smartphone size limit, manufacturers must adhere to this rule, which effectively keeps most smartphones' batteries below 20Wh or around 5,000mAh to comply. 


This is the reason brands are going to make slim phones and can't make phones with larger batteries.

author: @mobgream
text: Hot take: The air is the best idea Apple has ever had in a while, why, you might ask? Because by doing this, next year's Apple should release an iPhone that does not have a battery. And sell the battery separately as a magsafe. What would this achieve? Well, for one, we can buy just the iphone and decide how thick we want it by buying the battery separately therefore deciding how much battery we need as well; second, we will have a much longer iphone lifespan because battery is one of the reason why we switch phone, battery degrade; and third, if we want to go offgrid, or the power is not an option, we buy another battery and now we have a working phone, we do not need to wait while charging, just swap it and good to go. Maybe the phone should have a small battery that will only be use while the switch is happening so we do not need the phone turn off and on again.

text: I would recommend staying away from cases, covers, or skins on the pro phones because of the vapor chamber cooling.  They wouldn't have implemented the additional cooling, including all the R&D cost; if, it wasn't necessary.  If you cover up the phone with a case, cover, or skin; then, you'll prevent the chassis of the phone from most effectively dissipating the heat to atmospheric and/or your skin.  I'd predict a measurable performance drop if a case, cover, or skin is utilized on the pro phone; meaning, you would most likely be better off with the regular 17 at that point.

author: @daltonparker7792
text:
I personally disagree with the statement that phones have stagnated. Not only have the modern phones stagnated, but they are regressing for the sake of profit. 

We had the green movement that justified phone companies no longer including stickers, headphones, and charger blocks in the box. They no longer have headphone jacks, no SIM card trays, no expandable storage, etc. that forces you to buy their cloud storage subscriptions, because while the cameras are better, that also means that photos and videos take up more storage space. They force you into buying their wireless headphones, because if you buy the cheap ones, youre going to lose out on all of the features that their first party wireless headphones give you. They no longer include charger blocks anymore, which is actually causing more pollution from shipments and packaging, but it also makes the devices cost more. Now they can ship more phones in bulk since the boxes are thinner, and they can now ship large quantities of chargers that they can charge $40 for. 

Now, they are making the phones thinner, which they are saying is for the sake of advancements in technology, but its plainly not. It’s to save costs and maximize profits. By making the phones smaller, sacrificing internal components, and making smaller batteries, they are saving on manufacturing costs, but are charging $1k+ for these new phones. With one phone, that doesn’t make a big difference. But when you’re selling millions of units, that adds up to litteal metric tons of weight that they are saving on shipping costs and material costs. Not only that, but now they can sell you the external battery for your severely downgraded phone, to make even more profit. Forget selling you the charger brick separately, now you will be buying the batteries separately. 

Mark my words, this is going to be the direction that phone companies move towards. They will make phones that will turn every single native feature into a premium upgrade. You want a bigger battery? Buy this magnetic external battery bank. You want to listen to music? Buy our wireless headphones, but if you buy another companies headphones, they wont be able to do any of the stuff they should because we dont support their devices. You want more storage? Pay for our subscription service that allows you to have more storage. Where will this parade of stripping parts to sell you back as premium upgrades and services stop?

author: @reldegartGi
text:
There is a practical battery size limit for smartphones sold in the USA due to regulations for transporting lithium-ion batteries, specifically 49 CFR 173.185, which limits each cell to a 20Wh (Watt-hour) capacity for ground transport. While there isn't a direct smartphone size limit, manufacturers must adhere to this rule, which effectively keeps most smartphones' batteries below 20Wh or around 5,000mAh to comply. 


This is the reason brands are going to make slim phones and can't make phones with larger batteries.

text: I think there’s something more interesting going on once you zoom out. Apple, since its inception, has always improved toward thinness. We saw it in the Mac, iPad, iPhone, and watch. Now Apple has begun to fully embrace the “pro” mantra. The MacBook Pros only compromise on thinness and weight, as do the new iPhone 17 pros, and the Apple Watch Ultra. The only pro item that hasn’t followed suit is the iPad. I think it will continue this way since the iPads whole value proposition lies in its flexibility/portability. But it’s interesting to see Apple building up cylos between the air products and the pro products.

author: @chiragbhat3007
text:
Has anyone noticed how it feels like someone at Apple sat down with their team and held a meeting titled: "How do we make sure the iPhone 17 Pro series doesn’t end up as the worst upgrade in Apple’s history?"

And the result? Remove the iPhone 17 Plus model — that way, the 17 Pro series feels more important. Now, if someone wants a bigger screen or longer battery life, they're forced to go for the Pro models.

Honestly, if Apple had released the 17 Plus this year, most people (about 95%) wouldn’t need the Pro series. The Plus model would've had everything 95% user actually cares about — a larger display and better battery life. But now, if you want just those two things, you're forced to pay for the Pro Max — even if you don’t care about the “Pro” features.

To those saying the Pro Max offers more — sure, but let’s be real. Years ago, the difference between base and Pro models was meaningful. Now, Apple is clearly out of ideas but still wants to keep the high price tag. So they play these games: removing the Plus model to protect Pro sales, locking popular features behind more expensive models, and pretending it’s all about innovation.

Instead of admitting they can’t justify four iPhones a year anymore, Apple could’ve just offered two: one compact and one large. Then maybe introduce a true foldable and call that the real Pro model. But they’re not interested in actual tech progress — only higher revenue.

They even introduced a new “Slim” model to sit awkwardly between the 17 and 17 Pro, just to make the Pro look more attractive. And of course, they bumped up the Pro’s price too. So now your choices are:
- Get the basic iPhone 17, or
- Pay a lot more for the Pro — even if all you wanted was a bigger battery and screen.

It’s clear Apple doesn’t want to be a leading tech innovator anymore. They want to be a luxury brand, charging premium prices for features that should be standard — keeping things exclusive, not accessible.

Now compare pricing. In the U.S., the iPhone 17 Pro Max is somewhat in line with the Galaxy S25 Ultra. But in India? The Pro Max is almost the same price as the upcoming Galaxy Fold 7. That’s crazy. For Indian buyers, the S25 Ultra is way cheaper than the Pro Max — and if someone has a Pro Max budget, it makes more sense to just spend a little extra and get the Fold 7, which is a real innovation leap.

People often say taxes cause this, but remember — both iPhones and Samsung phones are now assembled in India. Neither is an Indian brand. So why is the price gap between them so much bigger in India than in the U.S.?

Honestly, I hope more Indians stop buying the Pro Max. Apple clearly isn’t thinking about users anymore — just profits. I’m not saying it should be cheap, but at least price it competitively with the S25 Ultra. Right now, Apple’s pricing in India makes zero sense

author: @RomansFiveDotEight
text:
This is a bit 'old man yells at cloud' but the unboxing used to feel a lot more special. It really, really set Apple apart. I was an early 'smartphone' adopter (Back when they were called 'PDA Phones'). My first iPhone was the iPhone 4. Those early PDA phones came with a ton of crap. You had docking stations/cradles, multiple cables, sometimes multiple batteries. I had one that even had hot-swappable battery capability, multiple batteries, and a battery charging dock so you could charge batteries outside the phone. But everything was always just sort of crammed in there with cardboard and bread ties.

But my first iPhone it was this sleek package with a neatly packed cable, a nice set of earpods with a really nice plastic case, documentation, stickers, a SIM eject tool, and of course the phone itself.

I'll be the first to admit that I literally have a drawer in my house dedicated to Apple charging bricks, cables, earpods, and the like. Because my family and I have accumulated so many. I even have several brand new USB 30-pin cables that have never been used! From various 30-pin based phones and iPods. (Remember the nice docks? I do, I miss them.) So I definitely don't need the junk. But I do miss that sort of 'like a kid at Christmas' feeling of having all these really premium feeling accessories in the box with the phone.

author: @minisainato
text:
iPhone Air battery life could be meaningfully improved by the C1X, I don't know why this was ignored. It has it, and even the Pro phones do not.

As compared with the iPhone 16, the iPhone 16e with the C1 chip (last gen, even less efficient), had 20% better battery, yet likely only about 10-15% was due to the increased battery and chip differences, meaning probably about 5-10% improvement was due to the first-gen C1 chip.

It wouldn't surprise me if the C1X delivers 10-15% better battery in real-world tests, and is an easy win for further incremental battery improvements on Pro lineup next year.

author: @JulianMous
text:
About the Air vs iPhone 16 battery difference:
Likely the Air rates way better than the iPhone 16 with video battery life, not because of the better chip but because the 16 lacks a promotion screen. The 16 needs to update the screen at 60 Hz with video while the Air adapts to video framerate like 24 Hz. This saves a lot of battery and explains why Apple always focuses so much on the video battery scores.

author: @DaxDoesDigital
text:
The baterry from Apple is only in white, but there are many ultra-thin MagSafe batteries that you could use on say the Space Black version, that are black in color. Even with the battery on, it's still thin! ...and it's not like you have to have the battery on the phone all day. You just top off the Air enough and take it off. But I think in my use-case, I won't need a MagSafe battery. The Air alone might surprise us on all-day usable battery life...

I think the black Air is the best for perhaps the fingerprints on the titanium to be less noticable. It's true, the Samsung Edge is very nice. If you are an Android user, look at that. But let's face it. It's comparing apples 🍎 to oranges 🍊😅 ...If you're in the Apple ecosystem, and want a thin phone, you're going to order the iPhone Air, not the Edge LOL 🤣 

It's great that Apple named it the iPhone Air, instead of including "17". I don't think it's they're planning on not releasing an updated version each year, but more  that the iPhone Air IS IN it's own category. Obviously, if you are a pro-user, get the Pro or Pro Max. If you are a regular-user, the iPhone 17 is the best value. But if you want something different, elegant, and doesn't feel like a brick in your pocket, get the iPhone Air....

author: @joshyang886
text:
They shamelessly put Steve’s quote “… Design is how it works.” to start the keynote. However the iPhone Air is perhaps the most crystal clear example of what “form over function” would mean. 
A 2025 smart phone to land the axes on perhaps the most important criteria of all. Battery, cameras, speakers! What a design decision they have made. For the same price, the iPhone Air would support none of those spacial imaging features they have been touting these recent years. It is a 26mm lens, with no ultra wide which Apple’s computational photography tends to rely on quite extensively. 26mm may not seem like a huge step downgrade from 24mm, but you can never crop out.
I don’t think battery and speakers need too much of an explanation to express the frustrations in this equation, but it really is a kick in the bottom of the phone, right back to the iPhone 7 era. What a throw back.
I’d like to quote Phil Schiller from way back “Can’t innovate my ass”.

So silly to measure phone thickness at its thinnest point.

If I have a 6mm slit can this "5.6mm" phone go through? Nope, because it's actually >9mm thick.

Fun fact, at >9mm the 2025 iPhone Air is thicker than:

Samsung Galaxy S2, from 2011 (which had a removable battery and headphone jack)
iPhone 5, 2012
Google Pixel 1st generation, 2016
Current 13" M4 iPad Pro

The point of the air battery is that you won't use it the whole time, only when you actually need it, ergo like every power banks that already exist. It's simply there to match the battery-lasting capabilities of the newer models so I don't see how that takes away from the phone being marketed as the "thinnest."

Additionally, I don't think any heavy phone user would buy the air, and I genuinely think the average phone user would use the separate battery less than they actually imagine.

author: @reldegartGi
text:
There is a practical battery size limit for smartphones sold in the USA due to regulations for transporting lithium-ion batteries, specifically 49 CFR 173.185, which limits each cell to a 20Wh (Watt-hour) capacity for ground transport. While there isn't a direct smartphone size limit, manufacturers must adhere to this rule, which effectively keeps most smartphones' batteries below 20Wh or around 5,000mAh to comply. 


This is the reason brands are going to make slim phones and can't make phones with larger batteries.

author: @jimmygreer2140
text:
Man, that Air is really a worthless hunk of junk. The processor is throttled to shit. Mr. Whose the Boss put them through the benchmarks and the BASE IPHONE IS BETTER than the chipset in the Air!!!

I wanted to know who is getting the Air and I had ONE person step up. I legit wanted to know what type of consumer would buy this phone. The person I talked to was basically like "I just need a phone" and had no real plans of taking photos with it (other than possibly like work type photos) wasn't gonna be doing any gaming on it. Bro just wanted something that is capable of communicating with his employers. He seemed to have given it quite a bit of thought so I wished him the best of luck & really hope it's not one of the things Apple regrets having produced.

Cause honestly, I don't have much hope for this phone. I call it the "Throttled iPhone" cause I imagine that's gonna basically be it's default setting. The INSTANT you try to do something even remotely taxing on the processor (taking videos, playing a legit video game) the phone is gonna throttle itself down almost IMMEDIATELY.

And I've just been laughing at CarterPCS on tiktok. Bro is trying to be the next big tech reviewer with a clear Apple bias. But bro is gonna show his ass with the iPhone Air.

author: @Allthecrazythings87
text:
I still have the iphone 11 so i was looking at an upgrade this year and thinking it would be nice to have a larger screen so i was seriously planning on getting the pro max. But in the back of my mind i kept thinking maybe it’s a bit overkill for me too. 

I get that the base iphone is the best value and that the air is losing out on some features but tbh… at this point ive made up my mind that im getting the air. Like think about the phone I have NOW and tell me it wont be a major upgrade in performance and battery AND camera no matter what. You cant. And… imma b honest with u… im a girl… i like pretty things. He said it right… that thing is like jewelry. Women like jewelry. A lot of these tech bros are always function over form but i know because i used to be a salesman for computers in the late 2000s… most users are very very vanilla. They dont need the longest battery. They dont need the best camera system. And if a person looking at the air and says “pretty” its valid. Its still a premium phone. You are 100% choosing form over function  but thats not a gamble. Theyre all solid choices unless your phone needs are more unique.

And imma add another layer to this… the air kind of does make a statement that has been trendier lately… it says… i dont depend on my phone. Im not chronically online or tech addicted. Its minimal… and that is kind of trendy. Trying to have less phone and tech rather than more. Thats a statement i think a lot of people… particularly women… will pay more for. 🤷🏼‍♀️ might seem silly, but its true.

text:
Honestly, I think we already have the champion of "best small phone of the year", being the iPhone 17 for sure.

I'm currently rockin a Galaxy S23 Ultra, and looking for a smaller phone, but sadly, here in Brazil, regular Galaxy S Series (not the Ultra) are powered by Exynos chips. Not that it would make big difference in daily usage, but it's hard to justify the price tag when you know elsewhere around the world people are having a better experience and probably better long-term reliability on their Snapdragon-powered phones.

So, with all that said, I'm truly considering going for the base iPhone 17. The only thing that kept me away from this was the 120hz screen, that thankfully is not a deal breaker anymore.

author: @Derpalerpa
text:
From Apple Insider - “The battery is rated at 3,149mAh, offering 12.26Wh. Compare that to the chonky original MagSafe Battery Pack, which was 1,460mAh but had a weird 7.62V voltage, so its effective rating was 2,920mAh or 11.13Wh.”

Very important to consider Whs one mAhs. It seems, however, that they’ve used a single cell battery rather than the two cell battery in series as the original MagSafe battery did. I believe they went this way to more easily achieve the necessary voltages for “high speed” wireless charging. 

Easier to step up from 7.62Vs than from 3.81Vs. Most likely, it’s more efficient. 

Would love to see and in depth review of the battery! Really wish they made some for the regular phones. Come  to think of it, the original MagSafe battery was likely made with the mini in mind, and this one with the air in mind. Still wish they went ahead and made one specifically for the larger phones. At least the OG MagSafe battery worked battery with the larger phones. It’s small size didn’t see too out of place on the back of a phone.

author: @El_Guap
text:
The original MagSafe Battery Pack (2021) had a higher internal voltage compared to the new iPhone Air version:
	•	Original MagSafe Battery Pack (2021)
	•	Capacity: 1,460 mAh
	•	Voltage: 7.62 V
	•	Energy: ~11.13 Wh
	•	(Apple achieved this by using two Li-ion cells in series, doubling the nominal voltage.)

New iPhone Air MagSafe Battery (2025)
	•	Capacity: 3,149 mAh
	•	Voltage: 3.89 V (nominal)
	•	Energy: ~12.26 Wh
	•	(Uses a single-cell design, closer to the iPhone’s own battery voltage.)

text: It often feels as though every positive mention of the iPhone is immediately followed by a “but” and a carefully stated critique. The result is that, point by point, the Android phone is always positioned as superior. Perhaps I’m misreading it, but the pattern gives the impression that Apple can never measure up, no matter what they do. What’s interesting is that this individual presents himself as unbiased toward Apple, yet the balance of commentary consistently tilts against them. Of course, he is a respected influencer—especially if one enjoys seeing Apple cast in a less favorable light.

author: @ItsZbow
text: I personally will be getting the Air. I think people forget that this is its own product, not meant to be the best of the best in the lineup. For me personally, I do not need all the camera functions that come on the other models as I only take photos to capture moments and memories in a snap. When it comes to the battery, I usually always have access to a charger of some sort at home, the car, work etc... Plus I do not use my phone for extensive things other then texting, calling, social media, and the occasional lowkey games. I doubt I will have any true problems with the battery life, and if I do end up getting the MagSafe charger it would be for some vacations or all-day activities and even so I doubt I will need it considering my phone usage. The thinness and weight is a bonus for me, considering all of the things I mentioned above!

author: @reldegartGi
text:
There is a practical battery size limit for smartphones sold in the USA due to regulations for transporting lithium-ion batteries, specifically 49 CFR 173.185, which limits each cell to a 20Wh (Watt-hour) capacity for ground transport. While there isn't a direct smartphone size limit, manufacturers must adhere to this rule, which effectively keeps most smartphones' batteries below 20Wh or around 5,000mAh to comply. 


This is the reason brands are going to make slim phones and can't make phones with larger batteries.

author: @natusMCMII
text:
5:49 Interesting. So the A19 chip in the regular iPhone 17 is faster than the A19 Pro in the iPhone Air, and the GPU performance is pretty much on par.
I guess this is due to things like thermal throttling because the extremely thin iPhone Air gets too hot otherwise?
Either way, this seals the deal: The regular iPhone 17 is the best bang-for-buck phone from Apple this year for 99% of customers, and it's not even close.

As long as you don't film, edit, and render 4K video footage on your phone daily, you won't _need_ the 17 Pro. I'm not judging the people that _want_ the best Apple has to offer (solely because they want the best), but they won't _need_ it is what I'm trying to say.
If you value design over everything else, sure, go ahead and buy the Air, but be aware that you can't put a case on in, otherwise you ruin the whole point of the phone being as thin as it is and you end up with a phone that's just as thick as the regular iPhone 17 while having worse performance, worse battery life, worse thermal management and a worse camera than the iPhone 17 while having paid almost as much as for the iPhone 17 Pro that doesn't have all these hardware restricitons.

All factors considered, the regular iPhone 17 is what the smart, not emotionally, and by marketing terms and FOMO influenced iPhone customer should buy if they want a new iPhone.

author: @ypuranik
text:
I last used an iPhone X, since then shifting to Android for good. However, this iPhone Air has truly hooked me, visually.

In India, shifting from a physical sim to an esim is a pain, it involves visiting the mobile connection providers customer care centre for converting the physical sim to esim vice versa, and/or moving esim to another mobile. Although the process is just a 5 minutes job, having to visit the Customer Care Centre is a pain. Thus the hesitation. 

I am not too much into making videos or clicking photos (only occasionally), but I do use my mobile for work related tasks, drafting mails, searching information, using ChatGPT and Google Gemini, a bit of youtube and literally no games! My Vivo X 200 Pro does a fantastic job and gives me a great battery life to do all of the above and more.

Please advice if the iPhone Air can be used as the main phone, or should I consider it as a second phone only (or completly ignore it)?

The iPhone 17 is absolutely amazing value for money this year, and has the better colours/design - but unfortunately my stupid monkey brain won’t let me settle for anything less than the Pro because of that telescopic lens and forged Aluminium unibody. 

I love how light the regular iPhones feel in comparison to the Pros - but I just can’t give up that extra camera lens. 💔

text: iPhone 17 Pro Max? Sure, if you enjoy paying $2000 for a camera bump that could moonlight as a satellite dish. But let me tell you, the iPhone Air is the most pointless gimmick Apple has pushed yet. It’s basically an iPhone Mini dressed up in a fancier name with slightly less power, targeting people who somehow think ‘Air’ makes it revolutionary. News flash: We don’t need lighter iPhones—we need batteries that last longer and real innovation instead of rehashing last year’s design. Apple’s just milking us while the rest of the industry quietly overtakes them.
